note_data.title: Biochemistry
keyPoints: Biochemistry is the branch of chemistry that deals with the chemical processes and substances in living organisms
Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones with general formula Cn(H2O)n
Proteins are complex organic compounds made up of amino acids joined by peptide bonds
Lipids are esters of fatty acids and glycerol, insoluble in water but soluble in organic solvents
Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed
Vitamins are organic compounds required in small amounts for normal growth and development
Nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) store and transmit genetic information in living cells
Carbohydrates provide energy (4 kcal/g), proteins build tissues, and lipids provide energy (9 kcal/g)

definitions.definition: The branch of chemistry that deals with the chemical processes and substances that occur within living organisms
Polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or compounds which yield these on hydrolysis with general formula Cn(H2O)n
Complex organic nitrogenous compounds made up of amino acids joined together by peptide bonds
A biological catalyst that increases the rate of biochemical reactions without itself being permanently altered
Esters of fatty acids and alcohols which are insoluble in water but soluble in organic solvents like ether and chloroform

importantQuestions.question: Define carbohydrates and give their general formula.
importantQuestions.answer: Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or compounds which yield these on hydrolysis. Their general formula is Cn(H2O)n where n is the number of carbon atoms.

importantQuestions.question: Write a detailed note on proteins including their structure, functions and sources.
importantQuestions.answer: Proteins are complex organic nitrogenous compounds made of amino acids. Structure: Primary (sequence), Secondary (α-helix, β-sheet), Tertiary (3D folding), Quaternary (multiple chains). Functions: Structural (collagen), Enzymatic (pepsin), Transport (hemoglobin), Defense (antibodies). Sources: Meat, fish, eggs, milk, pulses. Complete proteins contain all essential amino acids while incomplete lack some essential amino acids.

mcqs.question: The general formula of carbohydrates is:
Which of the following is NOT a function of proteins?
The bond between amino acids in proteins is called:

commonMistakes: Students confuse the general formula of carbohydrates - remember it's Cn(H2O)n, not CnH2nOn
Writing 'amino acid' instead of 'amino acids' when defining proteins
Forgetting to mention 'peptide bonds' when discussing protein structure
Mixing up functions of different biomolecules - carbohydrates for energy, proteins for structure
Not writing complete definitions word-for-word as given in STBB textbook
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Memorize exact definitions as they appear in STBB textbook - marks deducted for paraphrasing
Classification of carbohydrates (monosaccharides, disaccharides, polysaccharides) is frequently asked
Functions of proteins and examples are common 5-mark questions
Difference between DNA and RNA often appears as short question
note_data.keyDiagram: Structure of amino acid showing amino group (-NH2), carboxyl group (-COOH), and R group attached to central carbon atom. This diagram frequently appears in BSEK exams when discussing protein structure.
